_readme: ALTERNATIVES LAYER — alternative pairings for expressions already in the bank.
The strict rule (one expression, one active entry) still governs bank/. This
file is where alternative partners live while that rule stands. Lifecycle:
proposed  → the base layer. An idea with a bridge and a walk. Anyone may add.
staged    → approved out of the base layer by the editor (statusBy + statusOn
required). Waiting for a graduation path.
rejected  → vetoed, with the reason kept so it is never re-proposed.
GRADUATION (leaving this file) happens one of two ways:
a) REPLACEMENT — the alternative replaces the anchor entry's current partner
(the old partner may be archived here as 'proposed' to preserve it), or
b) SIBLING ENTRY — only if the parked spaced-repetition policy is revived
(see TODO standing decisions); the alternative becomes a new bank entry.
Fields per alternative:
anchor      — bank entry id this proposes an alternative FOR
keepSide    — which side of the anchor stays ('es' or 'it')
alt         — the proposed new partner: text, literal, meaning (fresh-authored)
bridge      — what THIS pairing teaches (must differ from the anchor's bridge)
walk        — the path from the anchor's current pairing to this one: what
changes, why both exist, when a learner needs which
status/statusBy/statusOn/notes — lifecycle bookkeeping

alt.meaning: To turn a situation around; to stage a genuine comeback.

alternatives.bridge: The straight pairing the frittata refuses to be: dar la vuelta a la tortilla and ribaltare la situazione BOTH mean a genuine reversal — this is the alignment Spanish speakers expect when they reach for the frittata and get spin instead.
alternatives.walk: Entry 0028 teaches the trap (frittata = manipulation); this pairing teaches the repair — the phrase 0028's own es→it warning tells you to use instead. Day one: the trap. Weeks later: the fix, remembered because the trap came first.
alternatives.notes: First natural spaced-repetition sibling; named inside 0028's interference text since 2026-07-13.

alt.meaning: To be fed up; to have had enough (neutral register, pan-regional).

alternatives.bridge: Averne fin sopra i capelli paired with the phrase a Mexican speaker actually says: estar harto. Loses the height-of-exasperation imagery, keeps the meaning and the ne-trap teaching intact.
alternatives.walk: 0026's es side (hasta las narices) is Peninsular-flagged and its MX idiomatic equivalent is vulgar. This alternative is option (b) from that entry's review question: re-pair for the LatAm-default audience and accept plainer color. If the Spanish reviewer rules for re-pairing, this GRADUATES BY REPLACEMENT and 'hasta las narices' gets archived here.
alternatives.notes: The regional re-pairing option, pre-drafted so the reviewer's ruling can be executed same-day.

alt.meaning: To be nuts; a bit crazy (Mexican colloquial).

alternatives.bridge: Fuori come un balcone next to Mexico's own looseness: zafado is a screw come loose where the Italian is a balcony jutting out — both architecture-adjacent madness, and the register finally matches on both sides.
alternatives.walk: 0002 is doubly flagged: Peninsular es side AND slang it side (the cautionary entry). This alternative fixes the regional half. Whether the balcony survives review at all is a separate question — if it dies, this alternative dies with it.
alternatives.notes: Depends on 0002 surviving Italian review; zafado register needs the Spanish reviewer.

alt.meaning: Break a leg — theatre-specific, deliberately crude.

alternatives.bridge: In bocca al lupo beside the only Spanish luck-wish that shares its jinx-dodging logic — but theatre-only and vulgar where the Italian is universal and polite.
alternatives.walk: This WAS 0039's original pairing, vetoed by the editor 2026-07-16: vulgar, and a scope mismatch (universal vs stage-only). Kept here as a rejected record so it is never innocently re-proposed. If a theatre-themed day ever exists, the veto could be revisited in that narrow context.
alternatives.notes: The layer's first inhabitant of the graveyard shelf — rejections are memory too.

alt.text: Non rimandare a domani quello che puoi fare oggi
alt.literal: do not postpone to tomorrow what you can do today
alt.meaning: Don't put off until tomorrow what you can do today.

alternatives.bridge: The literal twin: same words, same order, same task-shifting frame as the Spanish. A labelled freebie rather than a lesson — useful for showing which crossings are safe.
alternatives.walk: Entry 0063 pairs the Spanish with the older 'chi ha tempo non aspetti tempo', where Italian moves TIME rather than the task. This alternative is the flat, exact rendering — the one a learner would guess. Graduating it would mean swapping 0063's Italian side and losing the divergence, so it more likely stays here as evidence that the interesting pairing was chosen over the obvious one.
alternatives.notes: Filed 2026-07-18 when 0063 was banked with the non-obvious pairing.

alt.literal: to look for hairs on the egg
alt.meaning: To nitpick; to find fault with everything.

alternatives.bridge: The literal twin of the Italian: cercare il pelo nell'uovo and buscarle pelos al huevo are the same hair and the same egg. Entry 0022 deliberately pairs the Italian with 'buscarle tres pies al gato' instead, because cat-legs versus egg-hair teaches more than an exact match.
alternatives.walk: 0022 chose divergence over identity. This alternative records that Spanish ALSO has the exact twin — useful if a reviewer thinks the tres pies pairing is forced, and useful as evidence the divergence was a choice.

alternatives.bridge: A harsher Spanish downpour than 0014's llover a cántaros: pitchers of water become falling spears. Pairs differently with the Italian, trading volume for violence.
alternatives.walk: 0014 pairs jugs with jugs. This alternative would pair weapons with water — a bigger gap and a sharper card, but Peninsular-leaning and possibly unknown in Mexico. Reviewer call.
alternatives.notes: Surfaced from the book 2026-07-21; MX currency doubtful.

alt.literal: to fall out of the nest
alt.meaning: To be completely taken aback by news.

alternatives.bridge: The TRUE Spanish equivalent of cadere dalle nuvole — both mean astonishment at unexpected news, and both drop the person from a height.
alternatives.walk: Entry 0044 deliberately pairs cadere dalle nuvole with 'como caído del cielo' to teach a false friend: nearly identical imagery, disjoint meanings. This alternative records the honest equivalent the book supplied. If a reviewer finds 0044's false-friend framing unfair, this is the straight pairing — but banking both would cost the trap, which is the more valuable card.
alternatives.notes: Surfaced from the book 2026-07-21. Strong evidence that 0044's bridge is correct: Spanish HAS a real equivalent, so 'caído del cielo' really is a false friend rather than a rough match.
